commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=d9d4728ce1efd76d99f02e5c9fbf106ee1641280)
commands.review_summary:
The PR changes `applyDoctorConfigMutation` to set `pendingChanges` false in repair mode and updates doctor repair sequencing tests to expect repaired mutations to have no pending changes.

PR surface: Source 0, Tests 0. Total 0 across 3 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows the patched false value flows into `finalizeDoctorConfigFlow`, which writes repaired config only when `shouldRepair && pendingChanges`; I did not run tests because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] The contributor needs to repair the writeback regression and provide their own real behavior proof; automation cannot satisfy the external contributor proof gate for this PR.

target.title: fix(doctor): only set pendingChanges=true when changes were previewed, not applied
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

After deeper analysis, this is **not a bug**. The original code with `pendingChanges: true` in `applyDoctorConfigMutation` is correct for both preview and repair modes.

## Analysis

The field comment in `DoctorConfigMutationState` defines `pendingChanges` as:

> "True when candidate differs from the **persisted/effective config path**."

The key word is **"persisted"** — meaning the config file on disk, NOT the in-memory `cfg` field.

- **Preview mode** (`shouldRepair: false`): `cfg` = original on-disk config, `candidate` = suggested new config → `pendingChanges: true` ✅
- **Repair mode** (`shouldRepair: true`): `cfg` = mutated in-memory config, but **NOT yet written to disk** → `candidate` (which equals `cfg` in memory) still differs from the **persisted** config on disk → `pendingChanges: true` ✅

The downstream consumer `finalizeDoctorConfigFlow` (line 34) uses `pendingChanges` to decide whether to write config to disk in repair mode. Setting it to `false` in repair mode would prevent `--fix` from writing the repaired config, which is the behavior the CI failure caught.

The `fixHints` logic correctly branches on `!params.shouldRepair` (line 37-40) because fixHints are user-facing instructions that only make sense in preview mode. But `pendingChanges` tracks whether the in-memory state has unpersisted changes, which remains true in both modes.

The original report confused `pendingChanges` as meaning "candidate != cfg in memory" rather than "candidate != persisted config on disk."

## Evidence

CI failure on the attempted fix confirmed the analysis: `doctor-config-flow.test.ts` test failed because `shouldWriteConfig` returned `false` after repair — config would never be written.

## User Impact

No user-visible impact. Original code is correct.

## Conclusion

Closing as not a bug.
target.author: wendy-chsy
